name: Ama-no-Murakumo
description: Ama-no-Murakumo, later known as Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi (草薙の剣, 'Grass-Cutting Sword'), is a legendary Japanese sword and one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan. Its origin is steeped in myth, tracing back to the storm god Susanoo. After being banished from the heavens, Susanoo encountered a grieving family tormented by the fearsome Yamata no Orochi, an eight-headed serpent. Susanoo cleverly intoxicated the serpent and, in its weakened state, slayed it. Inside the serpent's tail, he discovered a magnificent sword, which he then presented to his sister, the sun goddess Amaterasu, as a gesture of reconciliation. The sword eventually passed into the hands of the Yamato Imperial family, solidifying its role as a symbol of imperial power and divine right. Its power is not merely in its sharpness but also in its association with courage, protection, and the ability to overcome great challenges. The sword is said to possess the ability to control the wind and is believed to be capable of warding off evil. The Kusanagi is currently enshrined at the Atsuta Shrine in Nagoya, although it is never publicly displayed. Its presence is a constant reminder of the divine origins and enduring strength of the Japanese imperial line, representing a potent link between the earthly and spiritual realms. The sword's absence from public view only adds to its mystique and reinforces its sacred nature. It is more than just a weapon; it is a national treasure and a powerful symbol of Japanese identity.
subtitle: The Grass-Cutting Sword of Legend
origin: Found inside the tail of the Yamata no Orochi by Susanoo, gifted to Amaterasu.

keyMyths.title: The Slaying of Yamata no Orochi
keyMyths.description: Susanoo slays the eight-headed serpent Yamata no Orochi and discovers the sword within its tail. He presents the sword to Amaterasu as a peace offering.

keyMyths.title: Yamato Takeru and the Burning Field
keyMyths.description: Prince Yamato Takeru used the Kusanagi to cut away the grass and set a counter-fire to escape a burning field. He realized the sword's power to control the wind in doing so.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: Translation of the Kojiki, a primary source for Japanese mythology.
sources.text: Translation of the Kojiki, a primary source for Japanese mythology.

sources.author: W. G. Aston
sources.description: Translation of the Nihon Shoki, another primary source for Japanese mythology.
sources.text: Translation of the Nihon Shoki, another primary source for Japanese mythology.

worshipPractices.name: Imperial Regalia Rituals
worshipPractices.description: As one of the Three Sacred Treasures, Ama-no-Murakumo (Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi) is involved in specific rituals surrounding the Imperial family. These rituals are highly secretive and involve symbolic gestures of power and legitimacy, reinforcing the emperor's divine right to rule. The sword itself is rarely, if ever, publicly displayed.

worshipPractices.name: Atsuta Shrine Devotion
worshipPractices.description: While not directly worshipped as a deity, Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi is enshrined at the Atsuta Shrine in Nagoya, where it is venerated. Pilgrims visit the shrine to pay respects and seek blessings associated with the sword's legendary power and protective qualities, though the actual sword is not visible.

festivals.name: Atsuta Festival
festivals.description: The Atsuta Festival celebrates the enshrined deities of Atsuta Shrine, including the spirit and history associated with Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi. The festival features processions, traditional music and dance, and offerings made to the deities to ensure continued prosperity and protection.

cultural.modernLegacy: Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi remains a potent symbol of imperial authority, national identity, and martial prowess in modern Japan. It frequently appears in anime, manga, video games, and literature as a powerful weapon or a symbol of destiny, reflecting its enduring cultural significance. Its association with the Imperial Regalia contributes to ongoing discussions about tradition and the monarchy's role in contemporary society.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Gram is a powerful sword with divine origins, reforged by a hero (Sigurd), similar to how Ama-no-Murakumo became a symbol of power and was wielded by heroes.
Excalibur is a legendary sword associated with kingship and destiny, much like Ama-no-Murakumo's role as a symbol of imperial authority and its connection to divine will.

Harpe, the sword used by Perseus to slay Medusa, possesses a unique attribute (adamantine blade or curved shape) that contributed to its wielder's success, similar to Ama-no-Murakumo's inherent power and ability to influence events.

associations.name: Wind
associations.significance: The sword's association with Susanoo, the storm god, links it to the power and unpredictability of the wind.

associations.name: Grass
associations.significance: The sword's later name, Kusanagi, directly references its ability to cut through grass, symbolizing its sharp power.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: White represents purity and sacredness, reflecting the sword's divine origin and status as a national treasure.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: The sword's ability to cut through anything can be associated with the destructive force and transformative power of fire.

associations.name: Authority
associations.significance: As one of the Three Sacred Treasures, the sword symbolizes the authority and legitimacy of the Japanese Imperial family.

associations.name: Serpent
associations.significance: The original form of Yamata no Orochi, the eight-headed serpent, is linked to the sword's origin, representing a triumph over chaos.

associations.name: Eight
associations.significance: Relates to the eight-headed serpent Yamata no Orochi, from whose body the sword was found.

companions.name: Susanoo-no-Mikoto
companions.description: Susanoo is the god who originally discovered and wielded Ama-no-Murakumo. He presented the sword to his sister Amaterasu as a peace offering.

companions.name: Yamata no Orochi
companions.description: The eight-headed serpent from whose tail Ama-no-Murakumo was extracted. While not a companion, the slaying of Orochi is intrinsically linked to the sword's discovery.

companions.name: Yamatotakeru
companions.description: A legendary prince who famously wielded Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi (Ama-no-Murakumo) and used it to escape a fire, renaming it Kusanagi ('Grass-Cutting Sword'). The sword became an invaluable tool in his heroic exploits.

companions.name: Miyasuhime
companions.description: One of Yamatotakeru's consorts, she indirectly played a role in his fate with the sword. She had a strong connection to Yamatotakeru's journey with the sword.

extendedContent.title: Creation & Forging: From Serpent's Depths to Imperial Regalia
extendedContent.content:
The tale of Ama-no-Murakumo begins with Susanoo-no-Mikoto, the storm god, banished from Takamagahara, the heavenly realm. Upon arriving in Izumo, he encountered an elderly couple and their daughter, Kushinada-hime, weeping over the impending doom brought by the Yamata no Orochi, an eight-headed serpent. Susanoo, promising to slay the beast in exchange for Kushinada-hime's hand in marriage (or, more accurately, her temporary transformation into a comb to be placed in his hair for safety), devised a plan. He prepared eight vats of strong sake, which the serpent greedily consumed, rendering it intoxicated and vulnerable. 

As the serpent slept, Susanoo drew his sword and began to dismember the creature. When he reached the serpent's tail, his blade struck something hard. Upon splitting the tail open, he discovered a magnificent sword within. This sword, initially unnamed, was later known as Ama-no-Murakumo, meaning "Sword of the Gathering Clouds of Heaven." This discovery is recounted in both the Kojiki and the Nihon Shoki, the earliest written records of Japanese mythology and history. 

Recognizing the sword's extraordinary nature, Susanoo presented it to Amaterasu-ōmikami, the sun goddess and his sister, as a gesture of reconciliation for his past misdeeds. This act of offering transformed the sword from a tool of conquest into a symbol of peace and reconciliation, solidifying its place within the imperial regalia and linking it to the divine lineage of the Japanese emperors.

extendedContent.title: Powers & Properties: Cutting Through More Than Just Grass
extendedContent.content:
Ama-no-Murakumo's name, later changed to Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi (Grass-Cutting Sword), hints at its exceptional sharpness and power. According to legend, during a hunting expedition, Prince Yamato Takeru was lured into a trap in a grassy field. His enemies set fire to the grass, intending to burn him alive. Yamato Takeru used Ama-no-Murakumo to cut down the surrounding grass, creating a firebreak. He then used the Kusanagi to mow down the remaining grass, allowing him to escape the trap. This event is directly responsible for the name change, highlighting the sword's ability to overcome adversity and protect its wielder.

Beyond its physical cutting ability, the sword is imbued with a sense of divine protection. It is believed to possess the power to control the weather, particularly the winds, and to bring victory in battle. Its presence is said to inspire courage and dispel fear in those who wield it. The sword's association with the storm god Susanoo further reinforces its connection to the elements and its capacity to influence the natural world. 

While the specific magical properties are not explicitly detailed in ancient texts, the sword's position as one of the Three Sacred Treasures implies a profound spiritual significance. Its power extends beyond the purely martial, representing the authority and legitimacy of the imperial line, and embodying the values of courage, resilience, and divine favor.

extendedContent.title: Notable Wielders: From Gods to Emperors
extendedContent.content:
The first wielder of Ama-no-Murakumo was, of course, Susanoo-no-Mikoto, who discovered it within the Yamata no Orochi. However, its most famous wielder is arguably Prince Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure whose exploits are detailed in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki. Yamato Takeru's use of the sword to escape the burning field solidified its reputation and led to its renaming as Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi. His story is a cornerstone of Japanese national identity, and the sword plays a central role in his heroic narrative.

After Yamato Takeru's death, the sword was enshrined at Atsuta Shrine in Nagoya, where it is believed to remain to this day. Although the sword has never been publicly displayed, it is said to be cared for by Shinto priests who perform rituals to maintain its power and sanctity. While Emperors do not traditionally wield the sword directly, its presence as one of the Three Sacred Treasures signifies their divine right to rule. 

The sword's continuous connection to the Imperial family, even through its enshrinement, ensures its ongoing significance. Its absence from public view only adds to its mystique and power, transforming it from a mere weapon into a potent symbol of national identity and imperial authority.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism: Authority, Legitimacy, and Divine Mandate
extendedContent.content:
Ama-no-Murakumo, as one of the Three Sacred Treasures (Sanshu no Jingi), represents the authority and legitimacy of the Japanese Imperial line. Together with the Yata no Kagami (mirror) and the Yasakani no Magatama (jewel), it symbolizes the key virtues associated with a just and effective ruler: valor (the sword), wisdom (the mirror), and benevolence (the jewel). The sword, in particular, embodies the strength and courage necessary to protect the nation and uphold justice. 

The sword's association with Susanoo, the storm god, further reinforces its connection to power and the ability to overcome chaos. Susanoo's slaying of the Yamata no Orochi represents the triumph of order over disorder, a theme that resonates throughout Japanese mythology and history. By offering the sword to Amaterasu, Susanoo demonstrated his respect for the established order and his willingness to reconcile with the heavenly realm.

The Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi's enshrinement at Atsuta Shrine also contributes to its symbolic significance. The shrine serves as a physical manifestation of the sword's power and its connection to the divine. Pilgrims visit the shrine to pay their respects and to seek blessings, further solidifying the sword's role as a symbol of national pride and spiritual significance. Ultimately, Ama-no-Murakumo represents not only imperial power but also the enduring values of Japanese culture: courage, resilience, and a deep connection to the divine.

shortDescription: A legendary sword from Japanese mythology, one of the Three Sacred Treasures. Originally found within the Yamata no Orochi, it's famed for its grass-cutting abilities.

wielders.name: Susanoo-no-Mikoto
wielders.context: Initial discoverer and wielder after slaying the Yamata no Orochi.

wielders.name: Yamato Takeru
wielders.context: A legendary prince who renamed the sword Kusanagi after using it to cut grass set ablaze by enemies.

wielders.name: Emperor Antoku
wielders.context: According to legend, the sword was lost at sea during the Battle of Dan-no-ura along with the emperor.
